skill.name: ai-video-generation
skill.description: Routes text-to-video, image-to-video, and video extension requests through the RunComfy CLI using documented model endpoints and prompt patterns.
skill.summary: Generate AI video clips with RunComfy model routing and CLI commands.
skill.author: agentspace-so

security_audit.summary: The high static risk score is mostly caused by Markdown examples, model endpoint URLs, and false positive weak-crypto matches. Real risks are limited to intended RunComfy CLI execution, outbound API use, user-provided media URLs, generated file downloads, and local token storage. No malicious intent, credential exfiltration, arbitrary shell pipeline, or prompt injection attempt was found in SKILL.md.

medium_findings.title: Intended CLI Execution Against External Service
medium_findings.description: The skill authorizes Bash(runcomfy *) and provides runcomfy run examples that send prompts and media references to RunComfy. This is the core function, but it can disclose prompt text and referenced media to the service if users provide sensitive inputs.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The command execution and outbound API behavior are explicitly documented in the skill. It appears intentional and scoped to runcomfy, with no evidence of arbitrary command construction.

medium_findings.title: Local API Token Storage
medium_findings.description: The security notes state that runcomfy login writes an API token under a hidden config directory in the user home directory. The documented 0600 mode reduces exposure, but the token remains sensitive filesystem state.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The token path and permissions are directly described on line 407. This is legitimate credential storage, not confirmed theft or exfiltration.

medium_findings.title: User-Provided Media URLs Enter Generation Pipeline
medium_findings.description: The skill supports image_url and audio_url inputs and warns that third-party media can influence generation. User-provided reference assets may contain private content or indirect instructions that affect generated output.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The media URL fields and indirect prompt injection warning are explicit. The risk is contextual because the URLs are expected inputs and the skill instructs agents to use only user-provided URLs.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Markdown CLI Examples Flagged as Ruby Backticks
low_findings.description: The static analyzer reported many Ruby or shell backtick execution findings. In context, these are Markdown inline code spans and fenced bash examples for the intended runcomfy CLI workflow, not Ruby backtick execution.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines are Markdown documentation and command examples. No Ruby file or Ruby execution context exists in the scanned file.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Weak Cryptography Pattern Matches
low_findings.description: The static analyzer reported weak cryptographic algorithm blockers at multiple lines. Manual review found model names, descriptions, tables, and links, with no hashing, encryption, or cryptographic API usage.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The referenced lines do not contain cryptographic code. The scanned skill is a Markdown router document, so these blocker findings are unsupported by semantic context.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded Documentation and Model URLs
low_findings.description: The skill includes many hardcoded RunComfy, docs.runcomfy.com, skills.sh, and example CDN URLs. These are expected catalog, documentation, and placeholder media references rather than hidden callbacks.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URLs are visible Markdown links or example inputs. Line 412 also documents an outbound allowlist, and no covert telemetry endpoint was found.

dangerous_patterns.title: Scoped Bash Tool Permission
dangerous_patterns.description: The front matter permits Bash(runcomfy *). This is narrower than unrestricted shell access, but it still allows a CLI that performs network requests, downloads files, and uses local credentials.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The allowed-tools declaration and operational description are explicit. The risk is bounded by the runcomfy prefix and the skill documentation.

dangerous_patterns.title: Generated Output Downloads to Local Directory
dangerous_patterns.description: The examples use --output-dir and the implementation notes say the CLI downloads generated media into that directory. This can write large files locally, though the skill documents a 2 GiB single-download cap.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: Multiple examples write to ./out, and the behavior is described in the How it works and Security sections. This is expected behavior, not a hidden write primitive.

content.value_statement: AI video model selection is complex and changes by input type, duration, audio, and quality needs. This skill routes requests to RunComfy models and provides matching prompts and CLI invocations.
actual_capabilities: Routes text-to-video, image-to-video, and video extension requests to documented RunComfy endpoints.
Recommends models based on quality, speed, audio, lip-sync, realism, aspect ratio, and duration needs.
Provides ready-to-run runcomfy CLI examples with JSON input bodies and output directories.
Documents prompting patterns for HappyHorse, Wan, Seedance, Veo, Kling, Hailuo, and Dreamina models.
Explains common workflows such as vertical social clips, product spins, cinematic ads, and dialog lip-sync.
Includes security notes for token handling, trusted installs, allowed endpoints, and generated file size limits.
limitations: Requires the RunComfy CLI and an authenticated RunComfy account or token.
Model availability, pricing, schemas, and output quality depend on the external RunComfy service.
Reference media URLs are sent to RunComfy and should not contain sensitive content without user consent.
The skill is documentation and routing guidance; it does not implement video models locally.

use_cases.title: Produce social video drafts
use_cases.description: Create vertical text-to-video or image-to-video clips with model choices for audio, duration, and fast iteration.

use_cases.title: Create product motion tests
use_cases.description: Animate product stills with physics-focused prompts, controlled camera movement, and suitable RunComfy endpoints.

use_cases.title: Plan cinematic AI video shots
use_cases.description: Select models for cinematic motion, multi-reference conditioning, character consistency, or video extension workflows.

prompt_templates.title: Generate a Simple Clip
prompt_templates.prompt: Generate an 8 second 16:9 video of [subject] doing [main action] in [setting]. Include natural motion and suitable ambient audio.

prompt_templates.title: Animate a Still Image
prompt_templates.prompt: Animate this image URL: [image_url]. Keep the subject identity stable. Add [specific motion], [camera instruction], and [audio direction].

prompt_templates.title: Choose the Best Model
prompt_templates.prompt: I need a video for [goal]. Inputs are [text, image, audio, or video]. Prioritize [quality, speed, cost, lip-sync, physics, or 4K]. Pick the RunComfy route and provide the CLI command.

prompt_templates.title: Design a Multi-Shot Sequence
prompt_templates.prompt: Build a multi-shot AI video plan for [campaign or scene]. Include model choice, shot beats, prompt language, aspect ratio, duration, audio approach, and RunComfy CLI invocation.

output_examples.input: Create a vertical social clip from a portrait with gentle motion and soft ambient sound.
output_examples.output: The skill would choose an image-to-video route such as HappyHorse I2V, preserve the portrait identity, set a vertical aspect ratio, describe the subtle movement, and save outputs to a local directory.

output_examples.input: Animate a product bottle so it rotates exactly 180 degrees with no background movement.
output_examples.output: The skill would recommend Veo image-to-video because the request needs strong object permanence and physics-respecting motion.

output_examples.input: Make a talking-head clip that matches a provided voiceover file.
output_examples.output: The skill would route to Wan 2-7 with an audio URL and a scene prompt describing the speaker, lighting, and camera framing.

best_practices: Confirm that users approve sending prompts and media URLs to RunComfy before running generation commands.
Lead prompts with the subject and main action, then add camera, motion, audio, and aspect ratio details.
Use faster or lower-cost model tiers for iteration, then switch to higher-quality endpoints for final renders.
anti_patterns: Do not paste RunComfy tokens into prompts, logs, examples, or generated documentation.
Do not use untrusted reference media unless the user explicitly provided it for the current task.
Do not choose a premium 4K or multi-modal model when a simple prompt or draft route is enough.

faq.question: Does this skill generate video locally?
faq.answer: No. It uses the RunComfy CLI to call external RunComfy model endpoints.

faq.question: Which tools can use this skill?
faq.answer: The report lists Claude, Codex, and Claude Code as supported tools.

faq.question: What inputs does it support?
faq.answer: It covers text prompts, image URLs, audio URLs for lip-sync, and video extension workflows.

faq.question: Does it need credentials?
faq.answer: Yes. Users must sign in with runcomfy login or provide RUNCOMFY_TOKEN in suitable environments.

faq.question: Is it safe to use with private media?
faq.answer: Use caution. Prompts and media URLs are sent to RunComfy, so private content needs explicit user approval.

faq.question: Can it pick a model for me?
faq.answer: Yes. It maps user intent to model routes for speed, quality, lip-sync, realism, audio, and extension needs.
